<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>
#include <reaction/graph/observer_graph.h>
#include <reaction/concurrency/thread_manager.h>
#include <memory>
#include <string>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// 数据模型定义
// ============================================

class TestObserver : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    TestObserver(const std::string& name) : m_name(name), m_changed(false) {}

    void valueChanged(bool changed = true) override {
        m_changed = changed;
        m_callCount++;
        // Call base class to propagate notification to observers
        ObserverNode::valueChanged(changed);
    }

    bool wasChanged() const { return m_changed; }
    int getCallCount() const { return m_callCount; }
    const std::string& getName() const { return m_name; }

private:
    std::string m_name;
    bool m_changed;
    int m_callCount = 0;
};

// ============================================
// 函数实现
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Create observer relationship and test notification
 *
 * @param observerName Name for the observer node
 * @param targetName Name for the target node
 * @return std::pair<bool, int> Pair of whether observer was notified and call count
 */
std::pair<bool, int> test_observer_notification(const std::string& observerName, const std::string& targetName) {
    // Create observer and target nodes
    auto observer = std::make_shared<TestObserver>(observerName);
    auto target = std::make_shared<TestObserver>(targetName);
    
    // Register nodes with the graph
    reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance().addNode(observer);
    reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance().addNode(target);
    
    // Add observer relationship (target observes observer)
    reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance().addObserver(observer, target);
    
    // Notify target of change
    target->valueChanged();
    
    // Return notification status and call count
    return {observer->wasChanged(), observer->getCallCount()};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/concurrency/thread_manager.h>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// 函数实现
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test thread manager registration and safety check
 *
 * @param enableSafety Whether to enable thread safety
 * @return bool Whether thread safety is enabled after operations
 */
bool test_thread_manager_registration(bool enableSafety) {
    // 1. Get thread manager instance
    reaction::ThreadManager &tm = reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance();
    
    // 2. Register current thread
    tm.registerThread();
    
    // 3. Enable thread safety if requested
    if (enableSafety) {
        tm.enableThreadSafety();
    }
    
    // 4. Return thread safety status
    return tm.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

   - `reaction::var`: to create a reactive variable.
   - `reaction::React::atomicAddAssign`: to perform an atomic addition.
   - `reaction::React::get`: to get the current value of the reactive variable.

 Steps:
  1. Include the necessary header (if not already included). However, note that the given code already includes <reaction/reaction.h>.
  2. Use `reaction::var` to create a reactive variable from `initialValue`.

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <memory>
#include <string>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// 函数实现
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test atomic operations on reactive variables
 *
 * @param initialValue Initial value for the reactive variable
 * @param addValue Value to add atomically
 * @return int The final value after atomic addition
 */
int test_atomic_operations(int initialValue, int addValue) {
    // 创建响应式变量
    auto reactiveVar = reaction::var(initialValue);
    
    // 执行原子加法操作
    reactiveVar.atomicAddAssign(addValue);
    
    // 获取最终值并返回
    return reactiveVar.get();
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>
#include <reaction/graph/observer_graph.h>
#include <memory>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// 函数实现
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test batch operations on observer nodes
 *
 * @return bool Whether batch operations work correctly
 */
bool test_batch_operations() {
    // 1. Create observer nodes
    auto node1 = reaction::ObserverNode::create();
    auto node2 = reaction::ObserverNode::create();
    
    // 2. Get observer graph instance
    auto& graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // 3. Create batch ID
    const void* batchId = graph.createBatchId();
    
    // 4. Register nodes to batch
    graph.addNodeToBatch(node1, batchId);
    graph.addNodeToBatch(node2, batchId);
    
    // 5. Verify nodes are in batch
    bool inBatch1 = node1->isInActiveBatch();
    bool inBatch2 = node2->isInActiveBatch();
    
    if (!inBatch1 || !inBatch2) {
        return false;
    }
    
    // 6. Unregister batch
    graph.unregisterActiveBatch(batchId);
    
    // 7. Verify nodes are no longer in batch
    bool notInBatch1 = !node1->isInActiveBatch();
    bool notInBatch2 = !node2->isInActiveBatch();
    
    // 8. Return success status
    return notInBatch1 && notInBatch2;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>
#include <reaction/graph/observer_graph.h>
#include <memory>
#include <string>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Data model definitions
// ============================================

class TestObserverReset : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    TestObserverReset(const std::string& name) : m_name(name), m_changed(false), m_callCount(0) {}

    void valueChanged(bool changed = true) override {
        m_changed = changed;
        m_callCount++;
        // Call base class to propagate notification to observers
        ObserverNode::valueChanged(changed);
    }

    bool wasChanged() const { return m_changed; }
    int getCallCount() const { return m_callCount; }
    const std::string& getName() const { return m_name; }

    void reset() {
        m_changed = false;
        m_callCount = 0;
    }

private:
    std::string m_name;
    bool m_changed;
    int m_callCount;
};

// ============================================
// Function implementations
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test observer reset functionality
 *
 * @param observerName Name for the observer node
 * @param targetName Name for the target node
 * @return bool Whether observer reset worked correctly (observer no longer notified after reset)
 */
bool test_observer_reset(const std::string& observerName, const std::string& targetName) {
    // Create observer and target nodes
    auto observerNode = std::make_shared<TestObserverReset>(observerName);
    auto targetNode = std::make_shared<TestObserverReset>(targetName);
    
    // Get observer graph instance
    auto& graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // Add observer relationship (target observes the observer)
    graph.addObserver(targetNode, observerNode);
    
    // Notify target and verify observer was notified
    targetNode->valueChanged(true);
    if (!observerNode->wasChanged() || observerNode->getCallCount() != 1) {
        return false;
    }
    
    // Record call count before reset
    int initialCallCount = observerNode->getCallCount();
    
    // Reset observer node
    observerNode->reset();
    
    // Notify target again
    targetNode->valueChanged(true);
    
    // Verify observer was not notified after reset
    if (observerNode->wasChanged() || observerNode->getCallCount() != initialCallCount) {
        return false;
    }
    
    return true;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>
#include <reaction/graph/observer_graph.h>
#include <memory>
#include <string>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Data model definitions
// ============================================

class TestObserverClosure : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    TestObserverClosure(const std::string& name) : m_name(name), m_changed(false) {}

    void valueChanged(bool changed = true) override {
        m_changed = changed;
        m_callCount++;
        // Call base class to propagate notification to observers
        ObserverNode::valueChanged(changed);
    }

    bool wasChanged() const { return m_changed; }
    int getCallCount() const { return m_callCount; }
    const std::string& getName() const { return m_name; }

private:
    std::string m_name;
    bool m_changed;
    int m_callCount = 0;
};

// ============================================
// Function implementations
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test node closure functionality
 *
 * @param nodeName Name for the observer node
 * @return bool Whether node closure worked correctly (relationships cleaned up)
 */
bool test_node_closure(const std::string& nodeName) {
    // Create observer nodes
    auto target = std::make_shared<TestObserverClosure>("target");
    auto observer = std::make_shared<TestObserverClosure>(nodeName);
    
    // Get observer graph instance
    auto& graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // Add observer relationship (observer observes target)
    graph.addOneObserver(target, observer);
    
    // Notify target and verify observer was notified
    target->notify(true);
    if (observer->getCallCount() != 1 || !observer->wasChanged()) {
        return false;
    }
    
    // Reset observer state
    observer->resetState();
    
    // Close observer node
    observer->close();
    
    // Notify target again
    target->notify(true);
    
    // Verify observer was not notified after closure
    if (observer->getCallCount() > 1 || observer->wasChanged()) {
        return false;
    }
    
    return true;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>
#include <reaction/graph/observer_graph.h>
#include <memory>
#include <string>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Data model definitions
// ============================================

class TestTransactionalObserver : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    TestTransactionalObserver(const std::string& name) : m_name(name), m_changed(false) {}

    void valueChanged(bool changed = true) override {
        m_changed = changed;
        m_callCount++;
        // Call base class to propagate notification to observers
        ObserverNode::valueChanged(changed);
    }

    bool wasChanged() const { return m_changed; }
    int getCallCount() const { return m_callCount; }
    const std::string& getName() const { return m_name; }

    void reset() {
        m_changed = false;
        m_callCount = 0;
    }

private:
    std::string m_name;
    bool m_changed;
    int m_callCount = 0;
};

// ============================================
// Function implementations
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test transactional observer updates
 *
 * @param nodeName Name for the node to update
 * @return bool Whether transactional update succeeded (no cycle created)
 */
bool test_transactional_update(const std::string& nodeName) {
    // Create observer nodes
    auto source = std::make_shared<TestTransactionalObserver>(nodeName);
    auto target = std::make_shared<TestTransactionalObserver>("target");
    
    // Get observer graph instance
    auto& graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // Set up initial relationship: source observes target
    graph.addObserver(source, target);
    
    // Notify target and verify relationship works
    target->valueChanged();
    if (!source->wasChanged()) {
        return false; // Source should have been notified
    }
    
    // Reset observer for next test
    source->reset();
    
    // Attempt transactional update that would create cycle
    bool cycleExceptionCaught = false;
    try {
        // Transactional update: source observes itself (creating cycle)
        source->updateObserversTransactional({source});
    } catch (const reaction::CycleException&) {
        cycleExceptionCaught = true;
    } catch (...) {
        // Other exceptions indicate test failure
        return false;
    }
    
    // Verify cycle exception was thrown
    if (!cycleExceptionCaught) {
        return false; // Expected cycle exception was not thrown
    }
    
    // Notify target again to verify original relationship preserved
    target->valueChanged();
    if (!source->wasChanged()) {
        return false; // Original relationship should still work
    }
    
    return true; // Test passed
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

 The function should:
  1. Create a target node.
  2. Create observer nodes.
  3. Get the observer graph instance.
  4. Add all observers to watch the target.
  5. Notify the target.
  6. Count notifications and verify all observers were notified.
  7. Return the total call count and notification status (whether all observers were notified).

 Steps in code:

 1. Create the target node by instantiating `TestMultipleObserver` with `targetName`.
 2. Create observer nodes by instantiating `TestMultipleObserver` for each name in `observerNames`.
 3. Get the singleton instance of the `ObserverGraph` using `reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance()`.
 4. For each observer node, add an observer relationship to the target node using `ObserverGraph::addObserver`.
 5. Notify the target node by calling `targetNode->notify()`.
 6. Check if each observer was notified (i.e., their `m_changed` flag is set to true) and count the total calls (sum of `getCallCount` for all observers).
 7. Return a pair: total call count (sum of all observers' call counts) and a boolean indicating if all observers were notified (all `m_changed` are true).

 However, note that the `TestMultipleObserver` class overrides `valueChanged` and increments `m_callCount` when notified.
 We can use the `getCallCount` method to get the number of times each observer was called.

 Important: We must reset the observers before the test to ensure a clean state.

 Let's write the code accordingly.
output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>
#include <reaction/graph/observer_graph.h>
#include <memory>
#include <string>
#include <vector>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Data model definitions
// ============================================

class TestMultipleObserver : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    TestMultipleObserver(const std::string& name) : m_name(name), m_changed(false) {}

    void valueChanged(bool changed = true) override {
        m_changed = changed;
        m_callCount++;
    }

    bool wasChanged() const { return m_changed; }
    int getCallCount() const { return m_callCount; }
    const std::string& getName() const { return m_name; }

    void reset() {
        m_changed = false;
        m_callCount = 0;
    }

private:
    std::string m_name;
    bool m_changed;
    int m_callCount = 0;
};

// ============================================
// Function implementations
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test multiple observers watching a single target
 *
 * @param targetName Name for the target node
 * @param observerNames Vector of names for observer nodes
 * @return std::pair<int, bool> Pair of total call count and whether all observers were notified
 */
std::pair<int, bool> test_multiple_observers(const std::string& targetName, const std::vector<std::string>& observerNames) {
    // Create target node
    auto target = std::make_shared<TestMultipleObserver>(targetName);
    
    // Create observer nodes
    std::vector<std::shared_ptr<TestMultipleObserver>> observers;
    observers.reserve(observerNames.size());
    for (const auto& name : observerNames) {
        observers.push_back(std::make_shared<TestMultipleObserver>(name));
    }
    
    // Get observer graph instance
    auto& graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // Add all observers to watch target
    for (auto& observer : observers) {
        graph.addObserver(target, observer);
    }
    
    // Notify target
    target->notify();
    
    // Count notifications and verify all observers were notified
    int totalCalls = 0;
    bool allNotified = true;
    for (auto& observer : observers) {
        totalCalls += observer->getCallCount();
        if (!observer->wasChanged()) {
            allNotified = false;
        }
    }
    
    return {totalCalls, allNotified};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>
#include <reaction/graph/observer_graph.h>
#include <memory>
#include <string>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Data model definitions
// ============================================

class TestChainedObserver : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    TestChainedObserver(const std::string& name) : m_name(name), m_changed(false) {}

    void valueChanged(bool changed = true) override {
        m_changed = changed;
        m_callCount++;
        // Call base class to propagate notification to observers
        ObserverNode::valueChanged(changed);
    }

    bool wasChanged() const { return m_changed; }
    int getCallCount() const { return m_callCount; }
    const std::string& getName() const { return m_name; }

    void reset() {
        m_changed = false;
        m_callCount = 0;
    }

private:
    std::string m_name;
    bool m_changed;
    int m_callCount = 0;
};

// ============================================
// Function implementations
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test chained observer relationships
 *
 * @param targetName Name for the target node
 * @param observer1Name Name for the first observer node
 * @param observer2Name Name for the second observer node (observes observer1)
 * @return std::pair<int, bool> Pair of total call count and whether chain notification worked correctly
 */
std::pair<int, bool> test_chained_observers(const std::string& targetName, 
                                            const std::string& observer1Name, 
                                            const std::string& observer2Name) {
    // Create observer nodes
    auto target = std::make_shared<TestChainedObserver>(targetName);
    auto observer1 = std::make_shared<TestChainedObserver>(observer1Name);
    auto observer2 = std::make_shared<TestChainedObserver>(observer2Name);
    
    // Reset nodes to ensure clean state
    target->reset();
    observer1->reset();
    observer2->reset();
    
    // Get observer graph instance
    auto& graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // Set up chain relationships
    graph.addNode(target);
    graph.addNode(observer1);
    graph.addNode(observer2);
    
    graph.addObserver(target, observer1);
    graph.addObserver(observer1, observer2);
    
    // Notify target
    target->valueChanged();
    
    // Verify both observers were notified
    bool chainWorked = true;
    
    // Check observer1
    if (observer1->getCallCount() != 1 || !observer1->wasChanged()) {
        chainWorked = false;
    }
    
    // Check observer2
    if (observer2->getCallCount() != 1 || !observer2->wasChanged()) {
        chainWorked = false;
    }
    
    // Check target (should be notified as well)
    if (target->getCallCount() != 1 || !target->wasChanged()) {
        chainWorked = false;
    }
    
    // Calculate total call count
    int totalCalls = target->getCallCount() + observer1->getCallCount() + observer2->getCallCount();
    
    return {totalCalls, chainWorked};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Function implementations
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test reactive expression using expr API
 *
 * @param a First input value
 * @param b Second input value
 * @return int The result of (a + b) * 2 using expr syntax
 */
int test_reactive_expression(int a, int b) {
    // Create reactive variables
    auto var_a = reaction::var(a);
    auto var_b = reaction::var(b);
    
    // Create reactive expression: (a + b) * 2
    auto expr_result = reaction::expr([&]() {
        return (var_a() + var_b()) * 2;
    });
    
    // Return the current value of the expression
    return expr_result.get();
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Function implementations
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test reactive action with side effects
 *
 * @param initialValue Initial value for the reactive variable
 * @param newValue New value to set
 * @return int The value captured by the action (should be newValue after update)
 */
int test_reactive_action(int initialValue, int newValue) {
    // 1. Create reactive variable
    auto reactive_var = reaction::var(initialValue);
    
    // 2. Create variable to capture side effects
    int captured_value = 0;
    
    // 3. Create reactive action
    auto reactive_action = reaction::action([&]() {
        // Update reactive variable (this will trigger the action)
        reactive_var.value(newValue);
        
        // Capture the value after update
        captured_value = reactive_var.get();
    });
    
    // 4. Update reactive variable (trigger the action)
    reactive_var.value(newValue);
    
    // 5. Return captured value
    return captured_value;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Function implementations
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test resetting a calculation node with new dependencies
 *
 * @param a First input value
 * @param b Second input value
 * @param c Third input value
 * @return int The result after resetting calculation to use different dependencies
 */
int test_calculation_reset(int a, int b, int c) {
    // Create reactive variables
    auto x = reaction::var(a);
    auto y = reaction::var(b);
    auto z = reaction::var(c);
    
    // Create initial calculation: sum of x and y
    auto calc = reaction::calc([&]() {
        return x.get() + y.get();
    });
    
    // Reset calculation with different dependencies: sum of y and z
    calc.reset([&]() {
        return y.get() + z.get();
    });
    
    // Return the result after reset
    return calc.get();
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <string>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Data model definitions
// ============================================

class PersonRecord : public reaction::FieldBase {
public:
    PersonRecord(const std::string& name, int age)
        : m_name(name), m_age(age) {}

    const std::string& getName() const { return m_name.get(); }
    int getAge() const { return m_age.get(); }

    void setName(const std::string& name) { m_name.value(name); }
    void setAge(int age) { m_age.value(age); }

private:
    reaction::Var<std::string> m_name;
    reaction::Var<int> m_age;
};

// ============================================
// Function implementations
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test reactive fields updating calculated info
 *
 * @param initialName Initial person name
 * @param initialAge Initial person age
 * @param newName Updated person name
 * @param newAge Updated person age
 * @return std::string Concatenated info after updates
 */
std::string test_reactive_fields(const std::string& initialName,
                                 int initialAge,
                                 const std::string& newName,
                                 int newAge) {
    // Create reactive PersonRecord variable
    auto person = reaction::var(PersonRecord{initialName, initialAge});
    
    // Create calculation depending on reactive fields
    auto info = reaction::calc([&person]() {
        return "Name: " + person->getName() + ", Age: " + std::to_string(person->getAge());
    });
    
    // Update name field
    person->setName(newName);
    
    // Update age field
    person->setAge(newAge);
    
    // Return updated info
    return info.get();
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Function implementations
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test naming reactive nodes and retrieving names
 *
 * @param valueA Initial value for varA
 * @param valueB Initial value for varB
 * @return std::string Combined string of retrieved names
 */
std::string test_named_reactions(int valueA, int valueB) {
    // Create reactive variables with names
    auto varA = reaction::var(valueA).setName("varA");
    auto varB = reaction::var(valueB).setName("varB");
    
    // Create calculations with names
    auto calcAB = reaction::calc([&varA, &varB]() {
        return varA() + varB();
    }).setName("calcAB");
    
    // Create action with name
    auto actionExample = reaction::action([&varA, &varB]() {
        return varA() * varB();
    }).setName("actionExample");
    
    // Retrieve names
    std::string nameVarA = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance().getName(varA.getPtr());
    std::string nameVarB = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance().getName(varB.getPtr());
    std::string nameCalc = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance().getName(calcAB.getPtr());
    std::string nameAction = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance().getName(actionExample.getPtr());
    
    // Return combined names string
    return nameVarA + "," + nameVarB + "," + nameCalc + "," + nameAction;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<think>We are given a function signature and a description of what it should do.
 We need to use the `reaction` library to implement the function.

 Steps:
 1. Create a reactive variable (sensor) with the initial value.
 2. Create variables to track the recomputation count and the last processed value.
 3. Create a filter-triggered calculation that depends on the sensor.
    The calculation should:
      - Check if the change in the sensor value exceeds a threshold (diffThreshold) since the last update.
      - If the change is significant, then check if the current value is above a levelThreshold to determine the status.
      - Update the recomputation count and the last processed value.
 4. Apply a filter to the calculation that only triggers when the change is significant (i.e., the difference is >= diffThreshold).
 5. Update the sensor with the first value and record the recompute count after this update.
 6. Update the sensor with the second value and record the recompute count after this update.
 7. Get the final status string.
 8. Return a tuple of (final status, recompute count after first update, recompute count after second update).

 However, note that the problem says:
   - The filter-triggered calculation should be set up to only recompute when the change is >= diffThreshold.
   - The calculation should also determine if the value is HIGH or NORMAL based on levelThreshold.

 Let's break down the calculation:

 We can have a reactive calculation that:
   - On each update, checks the difference between the current value and the last processed value.
   - If the difference is >= diffThreshold, then it updates the last processed value and increments the recompute count.
   - Then, it determines the status (HIGH or NORMAL) based on whether the current value is >= levelThreshold.

 But note: the filter should be applied so that the calculation only runs when the difference is >= diffThreshold.

 How to use the reaction library?

 We are given the following functions from the reaction library:
   - `reaction::var`: to create a reactive variable (the sensor).
   - `reaction::calc`: to create a reactive calculation.
   - `reaction::React::filter`: to apply a filter to the calculation.
   - `reaction::React::value`: to update the value of the reactive variable.
   - `reaction::React::get`: to get the current value of a reactive expression.

 We also have the `React` class which has methods for filtering and setting values.

 Implementation plan:

 1. Create a reactive variable `sensor` with `initialValue`.
 2. Create two reactive variables to track the recompute count and last processed value.
    We can use `reaction::var` for these as well.

 3. Create a calculation that depends on `sensor` and does the following:
      - It will have access to the current value of `sensor`.
      - It will also have access to the last processed value (which we can store in a reactive variable).
      - It will compute the difference between the current sensor value and the last processed value.
      - If the difference is >= `diffThreshold`, then:
          - Update the last processed value to the current sensor value.
          - Increment the recompute count.
          - Then, determine the status (HIGH or NORMAL) based on whether the current value is >= `levelThreshold`.
      - Otherwise, just return the previous status (or maybe not? We need to think about the flow).

 4. We need to apply a filter to the calculation so that it only runs when the difference is >= `diffThreshold`.

 5. We need to update the sensor with `firstUpdate` and then record the recompute count after that.
 6. Then update with `secondUpdate` and record again.

 7. Finally, get the current status and return the tuple.

 However, note that the calculation must be set up to update the recompute count and last processed value only when the filter passes.

 Let's write the code accordingly.

 Important: The calculation must be a reactive expression that updates when the sensor changes, but only if the filter condition is met.

 We can structure the calculation as follows:

   auto statusCalc = reaction::calc([&]() {
        double current = sensor.get();
        double last = lastProcessed.get();
        double diff = std::abs(current - last);

        if (diff >= diffThreshold) {
            lastProcessed.value(current);
            recomputeCount.value(recomputeCount.get() + 1);
            if (current >= levelThreshold) {
                return "HIGH";
            } else {
                return "NORMAL";
            }
        } else {
            // If the difference is below threshold, return the previous status?
            // But we don't have a way to get the previous status without storing it.
            // Alternatively, we can return the same status as before by not changing the lastProcessed and recomputeCount.
            // However, the problem does not specify what to do in this case.
            // Since the filter is applied, the calculation will not run unless the filter passes.
            // So in the else case, the calculation won't run, and the status remains unchanged.
            // But we need to return the current status. How?

            // We can have a separate reactive variable for the status and update it only when the filter passes.
            // Alternatively, we can have the calculation always return the current status, but then we would have to update the status even when the filter doesn't pass.

            // Let's reconsider: the calculation should only update the status when the filter passes. Otherwise, it should return the previous status.

            // We can have a variable `currentStatus` that is updated only when the filter passes. Otherwise, we return the previous value.

            // But note: the calculation must return a value every time it is called (even if the filter doesn't pass?).

            // Actually, the filter is applied to the calculation, meaning that the calculation will only be triggered when the filter passes.

            // So the calculation will only run when the filter passes. Therefore, the else case is not executed because the calculation is not run.

            // But we need to have a status that is available at all times. So we must have a default status.

            // Alternatively, we can have the calculation always return the current status, but then we would have to update the status even when the filter doesn't pass.

            // Let's change the approach: we can have the calculation always return the current status, but we only update the recompute count and last processed value when the filter passes.

            // However, the problem says: "Create filter-triggered calculation". So the calculation should only recompute when the filter passes.

            // We can have the calculation return the status, and we can set up the calculation to update the status only when the filter passes.

            // But note: the calculation function is called every time the sensor changes, but the filter determines whether the calculation's result is propagated.

            // We want the status to be updated only when the filter passes. So we can have the calculation update the status variable only when the filter passes.

            // However, the reaction library might not allow updating a variable inside the calculation unless the calculation is set up to run.

            // Let's look at the provided code for `calc` and `filter`:

            // The `calc` function creates a reactive expression that is updated when its dependencies change. The `filter` method of the `React` object (returned by `calc`) sets a condition that must be met for the calculation to run.

            // So we can do:

            //   auto statusVar = reaction::var(std::string{}); // initial empty string?
            //   auto statusCalc = reaction::calc([&]() {
            //       double current = sensor.get();
            //       double last = lastProcessed.get();
            //       double diff = std::abs(current - last);
            //       if (diff >= diffThreshold) {
            //           lastProcessed.value(current);
            //           recomputeCount.value(recomputeCount.get() + 1);
            //           if (current >= levelThreshold) {
            //               return "HIGH";
            //           } else {
            //               return "NORMAL";
            //           }
            //       } else {
            //           return statusVar.get(); // return the previous status
            //       }
            //   });

#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <tuple>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Function implementations
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test filter trigger with conditional recomputation
 *
 * @param initialValue Initial sensor value
 * @param firstUpdate First update (expected to be within threshold)
 * @param secondUpdate Second update (expected to exceed threshold)
 * @param diffThreshold Minimum delta to trigger recomputation
 * @param levelThreshold Threshold to categorize HIGH vs NORMAL
 * @return std::tuple<std::string, int, int>
 *         {final status string, recompute count after first update, recompute count after second update}
 */
std::tuple<std::string, int, int> test_filter_trigger(double initialValue,
                                                      double firstUpdate,
                                                      double secondUpdate,
                                                      double diffThreshold,
                                                      double levelThreshold) {
    // 1. Create reactive variable for sensor
    auto sensor = reaction::var(initialValue);
    
    // 2. Track recomputation count and last processed value
    auto recomputeCount = reaction::var(0);
    auto lastProcessed = reaction::var(initialValue);
    
    // 3. Create filter-triggered calculation
    auto statusCalc = reaction::calc([&]() {
        double current = sensor.get();
        double last = lastProcessed.get();
        double diff = std::abs(current - last);
        
        // Update last processed and count only if filter passes
        if (diff >= diffThreshold) {
            lastProcessed.value(current);
            recomputeCount.value(recomputeCount.get() + 1);
            
            // Determine status based on level threshold
            if (current >= levelThreshold) {
                return "HIGH";
            } else {
                return "NORMAL";
            }
        }
        
        // Return previous status if filter doesn't trigger
        return lastProcessed.get() >= levelThreshold ? "HIGH" : "NORMAL";
    });
    
    // 4. Apply filter to calculation
    statusCalc.filter([&]() {
        double current = sensor.get();
        double last = lastProcessed.get();
        return std::abs(current - last) >= diffThreshold;
    });
    
    // 5. Update sensor with first value
    sensor.value(firstUpdate);
    
    // 6. Record recompute count after first update
    int countAfterFirst = recomputeCount.get();
    
    // 7. Update sensor with second value
    sensor.value(secondUpdate);
    
    // 8. Record recompute count after second update
    int countAfterSecond = recomputeCount.get();
    
    // 9. Get final status
    std::string finalStatus = statusCalc.get();
    
    // 10. Return results
    return std::make_tuple(finalStatus, countAfterFirst, countAfterSecond);
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

 Steps:
 1. Create a ProfileFields object with initialScore and initialStatus (we'll use true for initialStatus as it's not provided).
 2. Create a FieldOwnerNode.
 3. Get the observer graph instance and register the owner node.
 4. Get the field graph instance and bind the fields of the ProfileFields to the owner node.
 5. Update the score field by adding scoreDelta and record the notification count after this update.
 6. Update the active field to false (or any change) and record the notification count after this update.
 7. Return the two notification counts in a FieldBindingResult.

 However, note that the provided code for the helper types (FieldOwnerNode and ProfileFields) is incomplete.
 We must complete the helper types as per the requirements.

 Let's complete the helper types first:

 For FieldOwnerNode:
   - We need to override valueChanged and increment m_notificationCount.
   - We need a method getNotificationCount to return the count.

 For ProfileFields:
   - It should be constructed with initialScore and initialStatus (we'll assume initialStatus is true if not provided).
   - We need to expose the score and active fields via methods.

 We are also given the FieldBindingResult struct.

 Implementation:

 We'll use the reaction library functions as described in the steps.

 Let's write the code accordingly.
output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Helper types
// ============================================

class FieldOwnerNode : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    void valueChanged(bool changed = true) override {
        m_notificationCount++;
    }
    
    int getNotificationCount() const {
        return m_notificationCount;
    }

private:
    int m_notificationCount = 0;
};

class ProfileFields : public reaction::FieldBase {
public:
    ProfileFields(int initialScore, bool initialStatus)
        : m_score(initialScore), m_active(initialStatus) {}
    
    reaction::Var<int>& score() { return m_score; }
    reaction::Var<bool>& active() { return m_active; }

private:
    reaction::Var<int> m_score;
    reaction::Var<bool> m_active;
};

struct FieldBindingResult {
    int notificationsAfterScoreUpdate;
    int notificationsAfterStatusUpdate;
};

// ============================================
// Function implementation
// ============================================

FieldBindingResult test_field_binding(int initialScore, int scoreDelta) {
    // Step 1: Create ProfileFields object
    ProfileFields profile(initialScore, true);
    
    // Step 2: Create FieldOwnerNode
    FieldOwnerNode ownerNode;
    
    // Step 3: Get observer graph instance and register owner node
    auto& observerGraph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    observerGraph.registerNode(&ownerNode);
    
    // Step 4: Get field graph instance and bind fields to owner node
    auto& fieldGraph = reaction::FieldGraph::getInstance();
    fieldGraph.bindFields(profile.getId(), &ownerNode);
    
    // Step 5: Update score field and record notification count
    profile.score() += scoreDelta;
    int notificationsAfterScore = ownerNode.getNotificationCount();
    
    // Step 6: Update active field and record notification count
    profile.active() = false;
    int notificationsAfterStatus = ownerNode.getNotificationCount();
    
    // Step 7: Return notification counts
    return FieldBindingResult{notificationsAfterScore, notificationsAfterStatus};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Helper types
// ============================================

class RecordingNode : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    void valueChanged(bool changed = true) override {
        m_notifications++;
    }
    
    int notificationCount() const {
        return m_notifications;
    }

private:
    int m_notifications = 0;
};

struct RollbackObservation {
    int notificationsAfterSecondBinding;
    int notificationsAfterRollback;
};

// ============================================
// Function implementation
// ============================================

RollbackObservation test_observer_state_rollback() {
    // Create RecordingNode instances
    auto sensor1 = std::make_shared<RecordingNode>();
    auto sensor2 = std::make_shared<RecordingNode>();
    
    // Get observer graph instance
    auto& graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // Register nodes
    graph.registerNode(sensor1);
    graph.registerNode(sensor2);
    
    // Set up initial dependency: sensor2 observes sensor1
    reaction::observe(sensor1, sensor2);
    
    // Save node state for rollback
    auto rollback = graph.saveNodeStateForRollback(sensor2);
    
    // Replace dependencies transactionally: sensor2 now observes itself
    graph.resetObservers(sensor2);
    reaction::observe(sensor2, sensor2);
    
    // Trigger notification on second sensor
    sensor2->valueChanged();
    
    // Record notification count after second binding
    int count_after_second_binding = sensor2->notificationCount();
    
    // Execute rollback
    rollback();
    
    // Trigger notification on first sensor
    sensor1->valueChanged();
    
    // Record notification count after rollback
    int count_after_rollback = sensor2->notificationCount();
    
    return {count_after_second_binding, count_after_rollback};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

CreateFactoryResult test_reactive_create_factory(int baseValue, int increment) {

        initialExprValue,
        updatedExprValue,
        squaredValue
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction

 8. Check if the direct observer is in the collected set.

 We are provided with the necessary headers and the structure of the `NamedObserverNode` and `ObserverCollectionSummary`.

 Steps in code:

 - We'll create three nodes: source, direct, and transitive.
 - Register them with the graph (using `ObserverGraph::getInstance().addNode`).
 - Set up the direct relationship: source observes direct.
 - Set up the transitive relationship: direct observes transitive.
 - Then, collect the observers of the source node.
 - Check if the direct and transitive nodes are in the collected set.

 Note: The `collectObservers` function returns a NodeSet (which is an unordered_set of weak pointers to ObserverNodes).
 We need to convert these weak pointers to shared pointers to check their names.

 However, note that the `NodeSet` is defined as `std::unordered_set<reaction::NodeWeak, ...>`. We can lock each weak pointer to get the shared pointer and then compare.

 But note: the `collectObservers` function is a member of `ObserverGraph` and returns a `NodeSet`. We are to use it.

 Implementation:

 Let's write the function accordingly.

 Important: We must include the necessary headers. Since the problem says to output the complete contents of the file, we must include the given code and then implement the function.

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <string>

namespace test_reaction {

class NamedObserverNode : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    explicit NamedObserverNode(std::string name) : m_name(std::move(name)) {}
    const std::string& name() const { return m_name; }

private:
    std::string m_name;
};

struct ObserverCollectionSummary {
    std::size_t totalObservers;
    bool containsDirectObserver;
    bool containsTransitiveObserver;
};

ObserverCollectionSummary test_observer_collection() {
    // Create nodes
    auto source = std::make_shared<NamedObserverNode>("source");
    auto direct = std::make_shared<NamedObserverNode>("direct");
    auto transitive = std::make_shared<NamedObserverNode>("transitive");
    
    // Get graph instance and register nodes
    auto& graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    graph.addNode(source);
    graph.addNode(direct);
    graph.addNode(transitive);
    
    // Set up direct and transitive relationships
    source->observe(direct);
    direct->observe(transitive);
    
    // Collect observers
    reaction::NodeSet observers;
    graph.collectObservers(source, observers);
    
    // Check results
    bool has_direct = false;
    bool has_transitive = false;
    std::size_t count = 0;
    
    for (const auto& wp : observers) {
        if (auto ptr = wp.lock()) {
            if (ptr->name() == "direct") {
                has_direct = true;
            } else if (ptr->name() == "transitive") {
                has_transitive = true;
            }
            count++;
        }
    }
    
    return ObserverCollectionSummary{
        .totalObservers = count,
        .containsDirectObserver = has_direct,
        .containsTransitiveObserver = has_transitive
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.reasoning:
<think>We are given the function signature and the expected behavior. We need to implement the function using the `reaction` library.

Steps:
1. Get the thread manager instance using `reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance()`.
2. Reset the thread manager state using `resetForTesting()`.
3. Register the current thread by calling `registerThread()` on the instance.
4. Get the thread count and safety status before creating the worker thread. We can use `getThreadCount()` and `isThreadSafetyEnabled()` methods.
5. Create a worker thread that also registers itself (by calling `reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance().registerThread()` inside the thread). We'll use a lambda for the thread function.
6. Wait for the worker thread to finish.
7. Get the thread count and safety status after the worker thread has finished.
8. Return a `ThreadSafetyStatus` struct with the collected data.

Note: We must ensure that the worker thread is properly created and joined.

Let's write the code accordingly.
output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/concurrency/thread_manager.h>
#include <thread>

namespace test_reaction {

struct ThreadSafetyStatus {
    bool safetyBefore;
    bool safetyAfter;
    std::size_t threadCountBefore;
    std::size_t threadCountAfter;
};

ThreadSafetyStatus test_thread_manager_multithread() {
    // 1. Get thread manager instance
    auto& tm = reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance();
    
    // 2. Reset thread manager state
    tm.resetForTesting();
    
    // 3. Register current thread
    tm.registerThread();
    
    // 4. Get thread count and safety status before
    bool safetyBefore = tm.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    std::size_t threadCountBefore = tm.getThreadCount();
    
    // 5. Create and start worker thread
    std::thread worker([&tm]() {
        // Worker thread registers itself
        tm.registerThread();
    });
    
    // 6. Wait for worker thread
    worker.join();
    
    // 7. Get thread count and safety status after
    bool safetyAfter = tm.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    std::size_t threadCountAfter = tm.getThreadCount();
    
    // 8. Return thread safety status
    return ThreadSafetyStatus{
        safetyBefore,
        safetyAfter,
        threadCountBefore,
        threadCountAfter
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct BatchLifecycleResult {
    bool closedInitially;
    bool closedAfterClose;
    int recomputeCountAfterExecute;
};

BatchLifecycleResult test_batch_manual_lifecycle(int initialValue, int updatedValue) {
    // 1. Create reactive variable
    auto reactiveVar = reaction::var(initialValue);
    
    // 2. Create variable to track recomputation count
    int recomputeCount = 0;
    
    // 3. Create calculation that depends on reactiveVar and increments recomputeCount
    auto calcResult = reaction::calc([&]() {
        recomputeCount++;
        return reactiveVar() * 2; // Example calculation
    });
    
    // 4. Create Batch object
    auto batchObj = reaction::batch([&]() {
        reactiveVar.value(updatedValue); // Update reactive variable within batch
    });
    
    // 5. Check if batch is closed initially
    bool closedInitially = batchObj.isClosed();
    
    // 6. Execute batch
    batchObj.execute();
    
    // 7. Record recompute count after execute
    int recomputeCountAfterExecute = recomputeCount;
    
    // 8. Close batch
    batchObj.close();
    
    // 9. Check if batch is closed after close
    bool closedAfterClose = batchObj.isClosed();
    
    // 10. Ensure calculation is evaluated (by checking recomputeCount >= 1)
    // 11. Return batch lifecycle result
    return BatchLifecycleResult{
        closedInitially,
        closedAfterClose,
        recomputeCountAfterExecute
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

        // Do nothing, just override the base class method.
    }
};

struct CacheStatsSnapshot {
    size_t graphEntriesBeforeCleanup;
    size_t graphEntriesAfterCleanup;
    double graphHitRatioAfterCleanup;
};

CacheStatsSnapshot test_observer_cache_stats() {
    // Create CacheProbeNode instances
    auto nodeA = std::make_shared<CacheProbeNode>();
    auto nodeB = std::make_shared<CacheProbeNode>();
    auto nodeC = std::make_shared<CacheProbeNode>();
    auto nodeD = std::make_shared<CacheProbeNode>();
    
    // Get observer graph instance
    auto& graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // Register nodes
    graph.registerNode(nodeA);
    graph.registerNode(nodeB);
    graph.registerNode(nodeC);
    graph.registerNode(nodeD);
    
    // Set up direct observer relationship
    nodeA->addOneObserver(nodeB);
    
    // Set up transitive observer relationship
    nodeB->addOneObserver(nodeC);
    nodeC->addOneObserver(nodeD);
    
    // Create NodeSet to store collected observers
    reaction::NodeSet observers;
    
    // Populate cache data by collecting observers
    graph.collectObservers(nodeA, observers);
    
    // Get cache statistics before cleanup
    auto statsBefore = graph.getCacheStats();
    
    // Trigger cache cleanup
    graph.triggerCacheCleanup();
    
    // Get cache statistics after cleanup
    auto statsAfter = graph.getCacheStats();
    
    // Calculate hit ratio after cleanup
    double hitRatio = 0.0;
    if (statsAfter.hitCount + statsAfter.missCount > 0) {
        hitRatio = static_cast<double>(statsAfter.hitCount) / 
                  (statsAfter.hitCount + statsAfter.missCount);
    }
    
    // Close all nodes
    nodeA->close();
    nodeB->close();
    nodeC->close();
    nodeD->close();
    
    // Return cache stats snapshot
    return CacheStatsSnapshot{
        .graphEntriesBeforeCleanup = statsBefore.totalEntries,
        .graphEntriesAfterCleanup = statsAfter.totalEntries,
        .graphHitRatioAfterCleanup = hitRatio
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct ConstVarResult {
    int initialSum;
    int updatedSum;
};

ConstVarResult test_const_var_propagation(int baseValue, int constValue, int delta) {
    // Create a mutable reactive variable
    auto mutableVar = reaction::var(baseValue);
    
    // Create a constant reactive variable
    auto constVar = reaction::constVar(constValue);
    
    // Create a calculation that sums both variables
    auto sumCalc = reaction::calc([&]() {
        return mutableVar.get() + constVar.get();
    });
    
    // Get the initial sum
    int initialSum = sumCalc.get();
    
    // Update the mutable reactive variable
    mutableVar.value(baseValue + delta);
    
    // Get the updated sum
    int updatedSum = sumCalc.get();
    
    return ConstVarResult{initialSum, updatedSum};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/concurrency/thread_manager.h>
#include <thread>

namespace test_reaction {

struct ThreadGuardState {
    bool safetyBeforeGuard;
    bool safetyAfterGuard;
    std::size_t threadCountBeforeGuard;
    std::size_t threadCountAfterGuard;
};

ThreadGuardState test_thread_guard() {

    auto& threadManager = reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance();
    
    // 2. Reset thread manager state
    threadManager.resetForTesting();
    
    // 3. Get thread safety status before guard
    bool safetyBefore = threadManager.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    
    // 4. Get thread count before guard
    std::size_t countBefore = threadManager.getThreadCount();
    
    // 5. Create a worker thread with ThreadRegistrationGuard
    std::thread worker([&]() {
        // ThreadRegistrationGuard will register the worker thread
        reaction::ThreadRegistrationGuard guard;
    });
    
    // 6. Wait for worker thread to complete
    worker.join();
    
    // 7. Get thread safety status after guard
    bool safetyAfter = threadManager.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    
    // 8. Get thread count after guard
    std::size_t countAfter = threadManager.getThreadCount();
    
    // 9. Return thread guard state
    return ThreadGuardState{

#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

class IdTrackedFields : public reaction::FieldBase {
public:

};

FieldIdComparison test_field_unique_id(int initialFirst, int initialSecond) {

    uint64_t firstIdSecond = first.getId();

    second.valueField().value(initialSecond + 1);

}

#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct LastHandleSnapshot {
    int initialValue;
    int afterUpdate;
    int afterSourceClose;
};

LastHandleSnapshot test_last_handle_restore(int initialValue, int updatedValue) {
    // Create a reactive variable
    auto sourceVar = reaction::var(initialValue);
    
    // Create a calculation with LastHandle policy
    auto calcNode = reaction::calc<reaction::LastHandle>([](int x) { return x * 2; }, sourceVar);
    
    // Get the initial result
    int initialResult = calcNode.get();
    
    // Update the source variable
    sourceVar.value(updatedValue);
    
    // Get the updated result
    int updatedResult = calcNode.get();
    
    // Close the source node
    sourceVar.close();
    
    // Get the result after source close
    int afterCloseResult = calcNode.get();
    
    // Return the snapshot
    return LastHandleSnapshot{
        .initialValue = initialResult,
        .afterUpdate = updatedResult,
        .afterSourceClose = afterCloseResult
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/id_generator.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct UniqueIdSequence {
    uint64_t firstValue;
    uint64_t secondValue;
    uint64_t thirdValue;
    bool strictlyIncreasing;
};

UniqueIdSequence test_unique_id_sequence() {
    // Create three UniqueID instances
    reaction::UniqueID id1;
    reaction::UniqueID id2;
    reaction::UniqueID id3;
    
    // Get values from each instance
    uint64_t first = id1.getValue();
    uint64_t second = id2.getValue();
    uint64_t third = id3.getValue();
    
    // Check if the sequence is strictly increasing
    bool increasing = (first < second) && (second < third);
    
    // Return the sequence with the values and the check result
    return UniqueIdSequence{first, second, third, increasing};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/resource.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct ResourceAtomicResult {
    int finalValue;
    bool operationReportedChange;
};

ResourceAtomicResult test_resource_atomic(int initialValue, int delta) {
    // Create a Resource instance with initial value
    reaction::Resource<int> resource(initialValue);
    
    // Variable to track if operation reported a change
    bool changeReported = false;
    
    // Perform atomic operation that adds delta to the value
    resource.atomicOperation([&delta](int& val) -> bool {
        val += delta;
        return true; // Change is always reported when we modify the value
    }, changeReported);
    
    // Get the final value
    int finalValue = resource.get();
    
    // Return the result
    return ResourceAtomicResult{finalValue, changeReported};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <array>
#include <cstddef>
#include <reaction/memory/sbo_resource.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct SmallPayload {
    int value;
    bool operator!=(const SmallPayload& other) const = default;
};

struct LargePayload {
    std::array<int, 64> data{};
    bool operator!=(const LargePayload& other) const = default;
};

struct SboStorageStatus {
    bool updateReportedChange;
    bool smallUsingSBO;
    std::size_t smallStackUsage;
    bool largeInitialized;
    bool largeUsingSBO;
};

SboStorageStatus test_sbo_storage_info(int initialSmall,
                                       int updatedSmall,
                                       int largeSeed) {
    // Create lambda to generate large payload
    auto createLargePayload = [](int seed) -> LargePayload {
        LargePayload payload;
        for (int i = 0; i < 64; ++i) {
            payload.data[i] = seed + i;
        }
        return payload;
    };
    
    // Create small SBOResource and initialize with initial value
    reaction::memory::SBOResource<SmallPayload> smallResource;
    smallResource.updateValue(SmallPayload{initialSmall});
    
    // Create large SBOResource without initialization
    reaction::memory::SBOResource<LargePayload> largeResource;
    
    // Update small resource with new value
    bool updateResult = smallResource.updateValue(SmallPayload{updatedSmall});
    
    // Get storage information for small resource
    auto smallInfo = smallResource.getStorageInfo();
    
    // Get storage information for large resource
    auto largeInfo = largeResource.getStorageInfo();
    
    // Populate status structure
    SboStorageStatus status;
    status.updateReportedChange = updateResult;
    status.smallUsingSBO = smallInfo.using_sbo;
    status.smallStackUsage = smallInfo.stack_usage;
    status.largeInitialized = largeInfo.initialized;
    status.largeUsingSBO = largeInfo.using_sbo;
    
    return status;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

 9. Exit guard scope (shared lock is automatically released)

 13. Create ConditionalSharedLockGuard in a scope block again (with thread safety enabled)
 14. Attempt to acquire unique lock while shared lock is held with safety
 15. Unlock unique lock if acquired
 16. Exit guard scope
 17. Return shared mutex result

 We note that the ConditionalSharedMutex is a type that conditionally locks based on thread safety being enabled.

 We are to use the following functions from the reaction library:
   - reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance()
   - reaction::ThreadManager::resetForTesting()
   - reaction::ConditionalSharedMutex (constructor, lock_shared, unlock_shared, try_lock_shared, lock, unlock, try_lock)
   - reaction::ConditionalSharedLockGuard (RAII guard for shared lock)

 However, note that the ConditionalSharedLockGuard is an RAII object that acquires the lock on construction and releases it on destruction.

 We must be cautious because when thread safety is disabled, the ConditionalSharedMutex does not actually lock, so try_lock_shared will return true.

 But note: the problem says "without safety" and "with safety". The "without safety" means thread safety is disabled, and "with safety" means enabled.

 Let's break down the steps and code accordingly.

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/concurrency/thread_manager.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct SharedMutexResult {
    bool trySharedWithoutSafety;
    bool tryUniqueWhileSharedWithoutSafety;
    bool trySharedWithSafety;
    bool tryUniqueWhileSharedWithSafety;
};

SharedMutexResult test_conditional_shared_mutex() {
    // Step 1: Get thread manager instance
    auto& threadManager = reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance();
    
    // Step 2: Reset thread manager state
    threadManager.resetForTesting();
    
    // Step 3: Create ConditionalSharedMutex instance
    reaction::ConditionalSharedMutex sharedMutex;
    
    // Step 4: Attempt to acquire shared lock without safety (thread safety disabled)
    bool trySharedWithoutSafety = sharedMutex.try_lock_shared();
    
    // Step 5: Unlock shared lock if acquired
    if (trySharedWithoutSafety) {
        sharedMutex.unlock_shared();
    }
    
    // Step 6: Create ConditionalSharedLockGuard in a scope block
    {
        reaction::ConditionalSharedLockGuard<reaction::ConditionalSharedMutex> guard(sharedMutex);
        
        // Step 7: Attempt to acquire unique lock while shared lock is held without safety
        bool tryUniqueWhileSharedWithoutSafety = sharedMutex.try_lock();
        
        // Step 8: Unlock unique lock if acquired
        if (tryUniqueWhileSharedWithoutSafety) {
            sharedMutex.unlock();
        }
    } // Step 9: Exit guard scope (shared lock is automatically released)
    
    // Step 10: Enable thread safety
    threadManager.enableThreadSafety();
    
    // Step 11: Attempt to acquire shared lock with safety (thread safety enabled)
    bool trySharedWithSafety = sharedMutex.try_lock_shared();
    
    // Step 12: Unlock shared lock if acquired
    if (trySharedWithSafety) {
        sharedMutex.unlock_shared();
    }
    
    // Step 13: Create ConditionalSharedLockGuard in a scope block again
    {
        reaction::ConditionalSharedLockGuard<reaction::ConditionalSharedMutex> guard(sharedMutex);
        
        // Step 14: Attempt to acquire unique lock while shared lock is held with safety
        bool tryUniqueWhileSharedWithSafety = sharedMutex.try_lock();
        
        // Step 15: Unlock unique lock if acquired
        if (tryUniqueWhileSharedWithSafety) {
            sharedMutex.unlock();
        }
    } // Step 16: Exit guard scope
    
    // Step 17: Return shared mutex result
    return SharedMutexResult{
        trySharedWithoutSafety,
        tryUniqueWhileSharedWithoutSafety,
        trySharedWithSafety,
        tryUniqueWhileSharedWithSafety
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/memory/memory_config.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct TinyPod {
    int a;
    int b;
};

struct MediumPod {
    double values[3];
};

struct LargePod {
    std::byte payload[128];
};

struct SboTraitSummary {
    bool tinyAlwaysSbo;
    bool tinyForceHeap;
    bool mediumConditionalSbo;
    bool mediumForceHeap;
    bool largeForceHeap;
    std::size_t alwaysThreshold;
    std::size_t conditionalThreshold;
    std::size_t maxStackUsage;
};

SboTraitSummary analyze_sbo_traits() {
    SboTraitSummary summary;
    
    // Get SBO traits for TinyPod
    using TinyTraits = reaction::memory::SBOTraits<TinyPod>;
    summary.tinyAlwaysSbo = TinyTraits::always_sbo;
    summary.tinyForceHeap = TinyTraits::force_heap;
    
    // Get SBO traits for MediumPod
    using MediumTraits = reaction::memory::SBOTraits<MediumPod>;
    summary.mediumConditionalSbo = MediumTraits::conditional_sbo;
    summary.mediumForceHeap = MediumTraits::force_heap;
    
    // Get SBO traits for LargePod
    using LargeTraits = reaction::memory::SBOTraits<LargePod>;
    summary.largeForceHeap = LargeTraits::force_heap;
    
    // Get SBO configuration thresholds
    auto config = reaction::memory::get_sbo_config();
    summary.alwaysThreshold = config.always_threshold;
    summary.conditionalThreshold = config.conditional_threshold;
    summary.maxStackUsage = config.max_stack_usage;
    
    return summary;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct ResetBehaviorSnapshot {
    int beforeResetValue;
    int afterResetValue;
    bool safetyEnabledDuringReset;
};

ResetBehaviorSnapshot test_react_reset_behavior(int initialValue, int multiplier) {
    // Step 1: Get thread manager instance
    auto& threadManager = reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance();
    
    // Step 2: Reset thread manager state
    threadManager.resetForTesting();
    
    // Step 3: Create a reactive variable
    auto reactiveVar = reaction::var(initialValue);
    
    // Step 4: Create a calculation
    auto calc = reaction::calc([&reactiveVar, multiplier]() {
        return reactiveVar.get() * multiplier;
    });
    
    // Step 5: Get value before reset
    int beforeResetValue = calc.get();
    
    // Step 6: Enable thread safety
    threadManager.enableThreadSafety();
    
    // Step 7: Check if thread safety is enabled
    bool safetyEnabled = threadManager.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    
    // Step 8: Reset calculation logic
    calc.reset([&reactiveVar, multiplier + 1]() {
        return reactiveVar.get() * (multiplier + 1);
    });
    
    // Step 9: Update source variable
    reactiveVar.value(initialValue + 1);
    
    // Step 10: Get value after reset
    int afterResetValue = calc.get();
    
    // Step 11: Return reset behavior snapshot
    return ResetBehaviorSnapshot{
        .beforeResetValue = beforeResetValue,
        .afterResetValue = afterResetValue,
        .safetyEnabledDuringReset = safetyEnabled
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction

namespace test_reaction {

std::vector<std::string> describe_error_codes(

#include <string>

namespace test_reaction {

struct ExceptionInfo {
    std::string formattedMessage;
    std::string originalMessage;
    std::string file;
    int line;
    std::string function;
    std::string codeName;
};

ExceptionInfo capture_exception_info(const std::string &message,
    reaction::ReactionException::ErrorCode code,
    const std::string &file,
    int line,
    const std::string &function) {
    // Create ReactionException instance
    reaction::ReactionException exception(message, code, file, line, function);
    
    // Populate ExceptionInfo struct
    ExceptionInfo info;
    info.formattedMessage = exception.getFormattedMessage();
    info.originalMessage = exception.getOriginalMessage();
    info.file = exception.getFile();
    info.line = exception.getLine();
    info.function = exception.getFunction();
    info.codeName = reaction::ReactionException::errorCodeToString(code);
    
    return info;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/resource.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct ResourcePointerSnapshot {
    int initialPointerValue;
    int pointerValueAfterWrite;
    bool pointerStable;
};

ResourcePointerSnapshot test_resource_pointer_view(int initialValue, int directWriteValue) {
    // Create Resource instance
    reaction::Resource<int> resource(initialValue);
    
    // Get first raw pointer
    int* firstPtr = resource.getRawPtr();
    
    // Read initial pointer value
    int initial = *firstPtr;
    
    // Write directly through pointer
    *firstPtr = directWriteValue;
    
    // Get second raw pointer
    int* secondPtr = resource.getRawPtr();
    
    // Read pointer value after write
    int afterWrite = *secondPtr;
    
    // Check if pointer is stable (same address)
    bool stable = (firstPtr == secondPtr);
    
    // Return resource pointer snapshot
    return ResourcePointerSnapshot{
        .initialPointerValue = initial,
        .pointerValueAfterWrite = afterWrite,
        .pointerStable = stable
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.reasoning:
<think>We are given a function stub and we need to implement it using the `reaction` library.
 The function should perform the following steps:

 1. Create a reactive variable (using `reaction::var`).
 2. Create a copy of the source (reactive variable) by using the copy constructor of the `React` object.
 3. Check validity before closing (using `operator bool` on both the original and the copy).
 4. Check equality before closing (using `operator ==` on both the original and the copy).
 5. Close the source node (using `close` method on the original).
 6. Check validity after closing (again using `operator bool` on both the original and the copy).
 7. Check equality after closing, but with exception handling (if an exception occurs during equality check, we set a flag indicating the check failed).

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct ValiditySnapshot {
    bool firstValidBeforeClose;
    bool firstValidAfterClose;
    bool copiesEqualBeforeClose;
    bool copiesEqualAfterClose;
    bool equalityCheckFailedAfterClose;
};

/**
 * Function: Demonstrate React::operator bool and operator== for validity tracking.
 *
 * @param initialValue Initial value stored inside the reactive variable
 * @return ValiditySnapshot Snapshot describing validity/equality before and after close
 */
inline ValiditySnapshot test_react_validity(int initialValue) {
    // 1. Create reactive variable
    auto source = reaction::var(initialValue);
    
    // 2. Create copy of source
    auto copy = source;
    
    // 3. Check validity before close
    bool firstValidBeforeClose = static_cast<bool>(source);
    
    // 4. Check equality before close
    bool copiesEqualBeforeClose = (source == copy);
    
    // 5. Close source node
    source.close();
    
    // 6. Check validity after close
    bool firstValidAfterClose = static_cast<bool>(source);
    
    // 7. Check equality after close with exception handling
    bool equalityCheckFailedAfterClose = false;
    bool copiesEqualAfterClose = false;
    try {
        copiesEqualAfterClose = (source == copy);
    } catch (...) {
        equalityCheckFailedAfterClose = true;
    }
    
    // 8. Return validity snapshot
    return ValiditySnapshot{
        firstValidBeforeClose,
        firstValidAfterClose,
        copiesEqualBeforeClose,
        copiesEqualAfterClose,
        equalityCheckFailedAfterClose
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <set>
#include <string>
#include <vector>

namespace test_reaction {

struct OrderingSnapshot {
    std::vector<std::string> orderedNames;
    bool allUnique;
};

/**
 * Function: Demonstrate React::operator< for ordering reactive handles.
 *
 * @param firstName Name assigned to the first reactive node
 * @param secondName Name assigned to the second reactive node
 * @param thirdName Name assigned to the third reactive node
 * @return OrderingSnapshot Snapshot of sorted names and uniqueness flag
 */
inline OrderingSnapshot test_react_ordering(const std::string &firstName,
                                            const std::string &secondName,
                                            const std::string &thirdName) {
    // Create reactive variables with assigned names
    auto var1 = reaction::var(0).setName(firstName);
    auto var2 = reaction::var(0).setName(secondName);
    auto var3 = reaction::var(0).setName(thirdName);
    
    // Insert into set to trigger ordering via operator<
    std::set<reaction::React> reactSet;
    reactSet.insert(var1);
    reactSet.insert(var2);
    reactSet.insert(var3);
    
    // Collect ordered names
    std::vector<std::string> orderedNames;
    orderedNames.reserve(reactSet.size());
    for (const auto &react : reactSet) {
        orderedNames.push_back(react.getName());
    }
    
    // Check uniqueness (should be true if all names are unique)
    bool allUnique = reactSet.size() == 3;
    
    return OrderingSnapshot{orderedNames, allUnique};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/memory/memory_config.h>
#include <reaction/memory/stack_monitor.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct StackAllocationSnapshot {
    bool smallAllowedWithoutMonitoring;
    bool largeAllowedWithoutMonitoring;
    bool runtimeAllowedWithoutMonitoring;
    bool largeAllowedWithMonitoring;
    bool guardSucceeded;
    bool allocationBlockedAtLimit;
    bool runtimeAllowedAfterRelease;
};

/**
 * Function: Demonstrate StackMonitor::canAllocate behaviour across modes.
 *
 * @param largeSize Value used for runtime canAllocate after release
 * @return StackAllocationSnapshot Snapshot of allocation checks before/after guard
 */
inline StackAllocationSnapshot test_stack_monitor_capacity(std::size_t largeSize) {
    StackAllocationSnapshot snapshot{};
    
    // 1. Disable monitoring
    reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().setMonitoringEnabled(false);
    
    // 2. Initialize stack monitor
    reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().initialize();
    
    // 3. Check allocation without monitoring
    snapshot.smallAllowedWithoutMonitoring = reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().canAllocate(reaction::SBOConfig::SMALL_SBO_SIZE);
    snapshot.largeAllowedWithoutMonitoring = reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().canAllocate(largeSize);
    snapshot.runtimeAllowedWithoutMonitoring = reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().canAllocate(largeSize);
    
    // 4. Enable monitoring
    reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().setMonitoringEnabled(true);
    
    // 5. Initialize stack monitor again
    reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().initialize();
    
    // 6. Check large allocation with monitoring
    snapshot.largeAllowedWithMonitoring = reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().canAllocate(largeSize);
    
    // 7. Create StackGuard in scope block
    bool guardSuccess = false;
    bool allocationBlocked = false;
    {
        reaction::StackGuard<reaction::StackMonitor> guard(largeSize);
        guardSuccess = guard.success();
        
        // 9. Check if allocation blocked at limit
        // Create another guard to test limit blocking
        reaction::StackGuard<reaction::StackMonitor> limitGuard(1);
        allocationBlocked = !limitGuard.success();
    }
    
    snapshot.guardSucceeded = guardSuccess;
    snapshot.allocationBlockedAtLimit = allocationBlocked;
    
    // 11. Check runtime allocation after release
    snapshot.runtimeAllowedAfterRelease = reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().canAllocate(largeSize);
    
    return snapshot;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <memory>

namespace test_reaction {

class TrackingFieldNode : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    explicit TrackingFieldNode(std::string name) : m_name(std::move(name)) {}
    
    void valueChanged(bool changed = true) override {
        m_notifications++;
    }
    
    int notifications() const {
        return m_notifications;
    }
    
    void clear() {
        m_notifications = 0;
    }

private:
    std::string m_name;
    int m_notifications = 0;
};

struct FieldGraphCleanupResult {
    bool notifiedBeforeDelete;
    bool notifiedAfterDelete;
};

inline FieldGraphCleanupResult test_field_graph_cleanup(uint64_t objectId) {
    // Step 1: Create owner and field nodes
    auto owner = std::make_shared<TrackingFieldNode>("owner");
    auto field = std::make_shared<TrackingFieldNode>("field");
    
    // Step 2: Get observer graph instance
    auto& observerGraph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // Step 3: Register nodes
    observerGraph.addNode(owner);
    observerGraph.addNode(field);
    
    // Step 4: Get field graph instance
    auto& fieldGraph = reaction::FieldGraph::getInstance();
    
    // Step 5: Register field nodes under object ID
    fieldGraph.addObjField(objectId, field);
    
    // Step 6: Bind field to owner
    observerGraph.addObserver(field, owner);
    
    // Step 7: Trigger notification on field
    field->notify();
    
    // Step 8: Check if owner was notified before delete
    bool notifiedBefore = (owner->notifications() > 0);
    
    // Step 9: Clear owner notification count
    owner->clear();
    
    // Step 10: Clear dependencies on owner
    observerGraph.clearNodeDependents(owner);
    
    // Step 11: Remove field mappings for object ID
    fieldGraph.deleteObjField(objectId);
    
    // Step 12: Attempt to bind field again
    observerGraph.addObserver(field, owner);
    
    // Step 13: Trigger notification on field again
    field->notify();
    
    // Step 14: Check if owner was notified after delete
    bool notifiedAfter = (owner->notifications() > 0);
    
    // Step 15: Close nodes
    // Nodes are managed by shared_ptr, so they will be automatically closed
    
    return FieldGraphCleanupResult{notifiedBefore, notifiedAfter};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>
